analysis_scope.goal: Identify non-memory-corruption CVEs from the json-c corpus that may still matter to a Rust reimplementation.
notes: Rust removes the memory-corruption issues in this corpus, but it does not automatically prevent algorithmic-complexity denial of service.
json-c objects are backed by a hash table, so collision behavior is still relevant if the Rust port uses a predictable hasher or preserves the original hashing strategy.
An idiomatic Rust implementation using a randomized default hasher for object keys would likely mitigate the only surviving relevant CVE.

relevant_cves.description: The hash functionality in json-c before 0.12 allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (CPU consumption) via crafted JSON data involving collisions.
relevant_cves.why_it_still_matters_in_rust: Rust does not prevent adversarial collision attacks against predictable hash tables. A Rust port that uses a deterministic or attacker-controllable hasher for JSON object keys can still degrade to worst-case CPU behavior during parse, lookup, insert, or compare operations.
relevant_cves.porting_guidance: Use a randomized, collision-resistant default hasher for object member maps, keep any fast deterministic hasher opt-in only, and add adversarial tests with many colliding keys. If insertion order matters, pair that requirement with an order-preserving map implementation whose default hasher is still randomized.
relevant_cves.local_source_note: The imported json-c source already salts string hashing with a random seed in original/linkhash.c, which indicates that upstream treated hash randomization as the fix direction.

excluded_cves.reason: Excluded because it is a primary memory-corruption bug: a buffer overflow in printbuf APIs.
excluded_cves.note: Rust's ownership and bounds checks should eliminate this specific bug class, though output-buffer growth logic still deserves ordinary correctness tests.

excluded_cves.reason: Excluded because the assigned CVE is primarily a memory-corruption issue: integer overflow leading to an out-of-bounds write in printbuf_memappend.
excluded_cves.note: The Rust port should still use checked arithmetic for buffer sizing, but this record is not part of the non-memory-corruption set.

excluded_cves.reason: Excluded because it is a stack-buffer-overflow in the auxiliary sample program json_parse.
excluded_cves.note: This is both memory-corruption and outside the core library API surface targeted by the Rust port.
